artifacts.content:
From: Melissa Thompson <mthompson@lisd.net>
To: Dr. Carla Nguyen <cnguyen@lisd.net>; SEL & Wellness Grant Review <wellnessgrants@lisd.net>
Date: 2026-03-05 7:14 AM
Subject: Implementation Timeline Clarification – Wellness/Makerspace Grant

Hi Carla and team,

Thanks again for meeting with me yesterday to talk through my wellness-focused makerspace proposal. I’ve updated the draft narrative and wanted to clarify the implementation dates before I submit the final version in EduGrants.

In the “Project Overview” section, I’m keeping the working title we discussed: "Staying After the Panic Attacks: Using Makerspace Routines at Lincoln to Manage My Fall 2023 Anxiety and Support Student Regulation." I tried to make sure the focus is on how those routines now support students’ emotional regulation, not just my own story.

For the timeline, I’ve aligned everything with the district pacing guides and our master schedule:
- Planning and material ordering: May–August 2026
- Staff mini-PD and student pre-surveys: last two weeks of August 2026
- Classroom implementation start date: 2026-09-01, with the first structured makerspace regulation routines piloted during 2nd-period 7th-grade science and my 8th-grade STEM elective.

Because my son is a 6th grader at Lincoln, I’m planning to make sure his class gets first access to the makerspace days this year so I can test things out with a group I know well before opening it up wider. I’ll note that transparently in the “Students Target Group Description” so it doesn’t look like I’m hiding anything.

Could you confirm that the 2026-09-01 start date works from a compliance standpoint for the grant cycle, and that it’s okay that full-scale rollout to all 6th–8th grades won’t happen until October?

If you’d like, I can swing by during your conference block today with the revised logic model.

Best,
Melissa

Melissa Anne Thompson
7th Grade Science & STEM Makerspace Lead
Lincoln Middle School, Lewisville ISD

metadata.recipient: Dr. Carla Nguyen <cnguyen@lisd.net>; SEL & Wellness Grant Review <wellnessgrants@lisd.net>

artifacts.content:
From: Melissa Thompson <mthompson@lisdlms.org>
To: STEM Cohort Team <stem_cohort@lisdlms.org>
Subject: Tentative start date for recycling prototype rollout
Date: 2026-05-03 06:41

Hi all,

I wanted to summarize what we agreed on during Friday’s PLC about the Community Solutions follow‑up projects, especially the recycling bin prototype that 7th grade will be piloting.

Right now, the working implementation start date for the classroom pilot is **August 26, 2026** (the first full week of school). That gives us a few days after teachers return for in‑service to re-label the bins, finalize the QR code tracking sheets, and train student “recycling captains.” If construction in the 700 hallway runs long, we could push the pilot to **September 8, 2026**, but let’s aim for the August date unless we hear otherwise from admin.

To stay realistic, we’ll treat the pilot as a soft launch through the end of the first six weeks. Data collection on contamination rates will start the same week we roll out the new bins. I’ll draft a parent communication to go out in the back‑to‑school newsletter so families know why the kids are suddenly obsessed with weighing trash.

Action items:
- Melissa: finalize student training slides by **August 15**
- Carlos: confirm custodial schedule changes
- Jenna: coordinate with PTA about signage printing

Reply‑all if you see any conflicts with **8/26** as our start date.

Thanks,
Melissa

metadata.subject: Tentative start date for recycling prototype rollout

artifacts.content:
From: Karen Ellis <kellis@lisdlms.org>
To: Melissa Thompson <mthompson@lisdlms.org>
Subject: Draft timeline for Community Solutions classroom integration
Date: 2026-04-28 11:07

Melissa,

Following up on our conversation about extending the Community Solutions Expo projects into next year’s curriculum, I put together a rough implementation timeline for when the redesigned units could actually start in classrooms.

My suggestion is that we treat the first semester as a planning and training window and officially begin implementation with students on **October 5, 2026**. That would be the date when all 7th grade science teachers shift into the new project-based sequence and start using the common rubrics and reflection tools you’ve been piloting.

Between now and then, we can:
- Use August in‑service days to train staff on the design thinking framework
- Run a small, voluntary trial in two sections starting **September 14** to work out any kinks
- Finalize assessment alignment and parent communication by the end of September

I know you originally floated an earlier start in mid‑September, but with the district’s new benchmark schedule and the ongoing HVAC work in the science wing, I’m worried we’ll overload everyone. If we anchor the implementation start date as **10/5**, we can message that clearly to families and the school board.

Let me know if this date conflicts with any of your makerspace carts or the ELL push‑in schedule.

Thanks for leading this work,
Karen

metadata.subject: Draft timeline for Community Solutions classroom integration

Full‑day working session to map out the implementation plan for the 2026–27 District STEM Innovation Grant.

Tentative implementation start date to propose to the board: **November 1, 2026**. This is when grant-funded activities (after‑school design clubs, family maker nights, and the shared mobile makerspace cart rotation) would officially begin on all three campuses. Classroom integration pieces may have earlier soft launches, but **11/1** will be the formal start date on grant paperwork.

This retreat is about setting the official grant implementation date; it does not lock in any separate internal project timelines Melissa may be managing.

artifacts.content:
From: Daniel Thompson <daniel.thompson@email.com>
To: Melissa Thompson <mthompson@lisdlms.org>
Subject: Your project start dates (because you’ll forget otherwise)
Date: 2026-08-10 21:18

Hey Mel,

You left your planner open on the kitchen table, so I snapped a pic and figured I’d type this out before we both lose track of which STEM thing starts when.

Here’s what you wrote under “Fall Implementations”:
- Classroom recycling pilot – starts **8/26/26**
- New advisory design challenge rotation – starts **9/15/26**
- After‑school Community Problem‑Solvers Club – first official session **9/22/26** (you underlined this three times)
- Makerspace family night series – first one **10/10/26**

You also scribbled “ask Karen about pushing main project launch to October??” in the margin, so I’m guessing nothing is set in stone yet. But at least this gives you a baseline when you’re answering all those district emails about implementation timelines.

Unrelated but important: Evan’s soccer practices start **8/29**, and Harper’s art club kicks off **9/12**, so please don’t schedule any giant launch days on those afternoons unless you want two very grumpy children.

Also, your mom called while you were grading. She sounded a little down about still needing rides to Plano for knee checkups, so maybe don’t stack big project start dates on her appointment days if you can avoid it.

Love,
Daniel

artifacts.content:
From: grants@lewisvilleisd.net
To: Melissa Thompson <mthompson@lisdlms.org>
Date: 2026-05-06 07:14 AM
Subject: Clarification Needed – Makerspace Grant Timeline

Hi Melissa,

Thank you again for submitting your “Mobile Community Makerspace” proposal. The review committee is excited about the way you’re tying ethical hacking, digital citizenship, and school safety into hands-on STEM work. The story you shared about your 7th graders from 3rd period in Fall 2024 – including your son Evan – getting two-day in-school suspensions after trying to 3D-print unauthorized key copies in the STEM lab really helped us understand why you want to channel that curiosity into supervised design challenges.

Before we finalize the award letter, we need clarification on your implementation start date. In the narrative you mentioned “launching in early fall 2026,” but the budget spreadsheet lists an implementation start date of **08/15/2026**, while your Gantt chart says “pilot push-in rotations begin October 2026.” For compliance, we have to lock one date into the grant agreement and it must be consistent across all documents.

Could you reply with the single date you want us to use as the official implementation start date for the project? Once we have that, we can route the paperwork for signatures and get your first disbursement scheduled.

Also, your note about personally spending about $1,200 on electronics kits and filament in 2023–24 (including the $600 you put on a 0% interest card after Daniel’s overtime was cut) resonated with several reviewers; they felt it underscored the need for this funding so you’re not subsidizing the program out of your own pocket.

Best,

Carla Nguyen
Grants & Innovative Programs
Lewisville ISD

metadata.subject: Clarification Needed – Makerspace Grant Timeline

artifacts.content:
Title: Draft Makerspace Implementation Timeline Work Session
Date: 2026-05-11 04:00 PM–05:30 PM
Location: Lincoln MS – Room 214 (STEM Lab)
Attendees: Melissa Thompson; Principal Harris; AP Lopez; Counselor Jimenez

Description:
Working meeting to hammer out the DRAFT implementation start date and overall rollout plan for the Mobile Community Makerspace grant.

Agenda:
- Review district grant office email requesting a single official implementation date.
- Compare options:
  • **Option A:** Implementation start date of 08/12/2026 (aligns with teacher workdays and allows time to inventory carts before students arrive).
  • **Option B:** Implementation start date of 08/24/2026 (first full week with regular bell schedule; easier for attendance/tracking).
  • **Option C:** Implementation start date of 10/03/2026 (delayed launch; allows extra time for PD but pushes back fall community event).
- Decide which date best matches projected staffing, PD, and equipment delivery.
- Map which grade levels will be first in the rotation, focusing on students who struggle to stay on task and sometimes get written up for behavior; this gives a structured way to keep some of the “frequent flyers” busy during the day.
- Confirm that any date we choose still allows time to prepare for a spring 2027 community expo.

Notes:
Melissa will bring:
- Updated Gantt chart with all three date options.
- Draft parent communication letter explaining when the makerspace will begin visiting classrooms.
- Behavior support data for target student groups.

metadata.title: Draft Makerspace Implementation Timeline Work Session

artifacts.content:
From: Principal Harris <kharris@lisdlms.org>
To: Melissa Thompson <mthompson@lisdlms.org>
Date: 2026-05-12 09:02 PM
Subject: RE: Makerspace Grant – Need to Lock in Start Date

Melissa,

Thanks for pulling together the options after our meeting. I agree that we should avoid a vague “early fall” description and pick a concrete implementation start date for the mobile makerspace.

Between the three dates you proposed, I’m leaning toward **August 18, 2026** as a compromise between your original idea of starting right on the first teacher workdays and my concern about giving everyone at least a couple of days with students before carts start rolling. If we list 08/18/26 in the grant paperwork, that still gives you time to run the first design challenge before Labor Day but doesn’t overload the first two days of school.

Please update your Gantt chart, the budget narrative, and the project description in the application portal to reflect 08/18/2026 as the official implementation start date. Once that’s done, send me the revised PDF so I can sign off and forward everything to Carla in Grants.

I also appreciated how you framed the backstory about the 3D-printed key incident and your out-of-pocket spending on electronics kits and filament; it makes a compelling case that this project will redirect student curiosity in a positive way and relieve some of the financial burden you’ve been carrying.

Let me know if you hit any snags updating the documents tonight.

– K. Harris
Principal, Lincoln Middle School

metadata.subject: RE: Makerspace Grant – Need to Lock in Start Date

artifacts.content:
From: Melissa Thompson <mthompson@lisd.net>
To: Dr. Karen Alvarez <kalvarez@lisd.net>, STEM Instruction Team <stem_cohort@lisd.net>
Date: 2026-02-23 06:18:00 -0600
Subject: Final Numbers & Session Description for Community Solutions Expo

Hi Karen and team,

I finally have the updated counts and description for our portion of the Community Solutions Expo so you can finalize the program and room assignments.

Across 6th–8th grade, we’ll have 240.0 students participating in the STEM showcase blocks I’m coordinating. That includes all three of my 7th-grade science sections, my 8th-grade STEM elective, and two 6th-grade classes that have been using the mobile makerspace this semester.

For the write-up, you can list my strand under the working title:

Project Title: "Second Chance STEM: Re-Engaging My 7th Period Students After the Spring 2024 Vaping Suspensions"

The focus is on how we used hands-on design challenges and community problem-solving to rebuild trust and motivation with a group that had a really rough spring last year. Several of those same students are now leading teams developing low-cost accessibility tools and classroom sustainability projects.

Logistics:
- Preferred location: main gym for the larger prototypes, plus two nearby classrooms for quieter reflection stations
- Ideal time slot: anytime between 5:30–7:00 p.m., since many of my families work later shifts
- Tech needs: 6 tables with outlets, access to the rolling projector cart, and Wi‑Fi that can handle a few microcontroller demos

Let me know if you need student name lists or if we should cap any group sizes for safety. I can also pull a short quote from a student if you’re planning a press release.

Thanks for pulling all of this together,

Melissa

Melissa Anne Thompson
7th Grade Science & STEM Makerspace Lead
Lincoln Middle School, Lewisville ISD

artifacts.content:
From: Melissa Thompson <mthompson@lisdlms.org>
To: Carla Nguyen <cnguyen@lisdlms.org>
Date: 2025-01-22 06:14 AM
Subject: Rough headcount for Community Solutions Expo planning

Hey Carla,

I’m trying to pull together a rough headcount for the Community Solutions Expo so I can finish the draft for the grant application I told you about (working title is "Closing the Gap After Daniel’s 3-Month Unemployment – Low-Cost STEM Makerspace Solutions for Underfunded Classrooms"). The grant form keeps asking for the number of students participating, and I don’t want to lowball it.

Here’s what I’ve got so far:
- My 7th-grade science classes: if everyone shows up, that’s about 86 kids.
- The 8th-grade STEM elective: 24 currently enrolled, but realistically I think only around 18 will follow through with full projects.
- The 6th-grade advisory pull-out group you and I share: I counted 32 on the roster, but several are hit-or-miss with attendance.

Right now I only have about 8–10 students who are genuinely interested; the rest of the kids I’m planning to include are the ones who got placed in my class because they needed somewhere to go during advisory and don’t really care about STEM. I’m hoping once we start prototyping, more of them will buy in.

Can you double-check the 6th-grade numbers and let me know what you’d feel comfortable putting down as our “expected participation” for planning purposes? I was thinking of estimating around 120 total, but I’d love your reality check before I send anything to admin.

Thanks,
Melissa

metadata.subject: Rough headcount for Community Solutions Expo planning

artifacts.content:
Title: Expo Planning – Student Participation Numbers
Date: 2025-02-03 4:00 PM–4:45 PM
Location: Lincoln MS – Room 214 (Makerspace Cart Hub)
Attendees: Melissa Thompson; Assistant Principal Reyes; Counselor Jordan; STEM Coach Patel

Agenda:
1. Confirm projected student participation for May 2025 Community Solutions Expo.
2. Align expo schedule with testing windows and band/choir concerts.
3. Discuss supports for students with anxiety and IEP accommodations during group work and presentations.

Notes:
- Melissa shared that, because her daughter Harper was diagnosed with generalized anxiety in Spring 2023 after several panic episodes during unstructured group work at Valley Ridge Elementary, she’s designing the mobile STEM makerspace project so that 6th–8th graders can choose between quiet, structured design roles and more public-facing presentation roles during the expo. She plans to consult briefly with Harper’s pediatric therapist, Dr. Angela Ruiz at Children’s Medical Center Plano, about scaffolding collaboration in a way that reduces anxiety triggers.
- Current working estimate for expo participation:
  • 7th-grade science sections: ~90 students
  • 8th-grade STEM elective: ~25 students
  • 6th-grade advisory-based groups: ~40 students
  • Potential “maybe” group from Ms. Salinas’s ESL block: 15–20 students
- Reyes suggested using a conservative planning number of 150–160 students for space and supervision purposes.
- Need to finalize a single number for the district event form by mid-March.
- Action item: Melissa will circulate a commitment form to students and families to narrow down the headcount before spring break.

From: AP Reyes <areyes@lisdlms.org>
To: Melissa Thompson <mthompson@lisdlms.org>
Date: 2025-02-10 09:31 AM
Subject: Re: Need a ballpark student count for Expo request form

Hi Melissa,

Thanks for sending the draft of the Community Solutions Expo request. I looked over the section where they ask for the number of students participating.

Based on last year’s STEM night, we had 112 students who actually presented, even though we originally projected closer to 140. Given that this year you’re pulling in all three grade levels and your mobile makerspace is visiting more classrooms, I think it’s safe to bump our projection.

You mentioned in your note that you expect:
- around 80–85 from your core 7th-grade classes,
- roughly 20 from the 8th-grade STEM elective,
- and possibly 30–35 from the rotating 6th-grade groups.

That would put us somewhere in the 130–140 range. For the district form, I suggest we list 135 students as the anticipated participation. It’s specific enough to look thoughtful but still flexible if a few groups drop out.

If the grant tied to "Closing the Gap After Daniel’s 3-Month Unemployment – Low-Cost STEM Makerspace Solutions for Underfunded Classrooms" is approved, we can always submit an updated count later when you have more concrete commitments.

Please update the request form with 135 and resubmit it by Thursday so I can sign off before it goes to the district events office.

Best,
Reyes

metadata.subject: Re: Need a ballpark student count for Expo request form

artifacts.content:
From: Melissa Thompson <mthompson@lisdlms.org>
To: 7th Grade Science PLC <7thscience@lisdlms.org>
Date: 2025-03-04 05:58 AM
Subject: Quick survey – how many kids are actually building for the Expo?

Good morning team,

As I’m finalizing the logistics for the Community Solutions Expo, I need a more realistic sense of how many students are actually committed to building and presenting projects versus just doing the in-class activities.

Right now, my best guess is:
- Period 1: 23 students on the roster, but only 15 have started prototypes.
- Period 3: 25 on the roster, 17 actively building.
- Period 5: 24 on the roster, about 14 genuinely engaged.
- STEM elective: 22 enrolled; 18 say they want to present, but we’ll see.

Across these, that gives me roughly 60–65 students who seem serious about presenting. I know more may jump in once we get closer, but I’d rather plan for the smaller number for table space, tri-fold boards, and supervision.

Can each of you reply with:
1) Total kids on your rosters who are technically part of the project, and
2) How many you realistically expect to present at the expo?

The grant reviewers are asking for a breakdown between "exposed to the project" and "active participants," so even ballpark figures are helpful.

Thanks for taking a minute this week to count heads. I’m trying not to over-order materials, especially since our budget is still in limbo.

– Melissa

metadata.subject: Quick survey – how many kids are actually building for the Expo?

Hey Amanda,

I just finished the draft of the Community Solutions Expo mini‑grant application for the district foundation and I need to lock in a student participation estimate before I submit.

Date: 2026-03-04 3:30 PM–4:30 PM
Location: Lincoln MS – Library Conference Room
Attendees: Melissa Thompson; Principal Karen Lee; Counselor James Patel; 6th Grade Rep (TBD); 8th Grade Rep (TBD)

Agenda:

1. Confirm projected student participation for Community Solutions Expo
   - Current working estimate: **approx. 225 students** across grades 6–8
   - Breakdown to review:
     • 6th grade: 60–70 students (two teams fully committed, one partial)
     • 7th grade: 90 students (all three of Melissa’s sections, minus a few students with conflicting activities)
     • 8th grade: 55–65 students (STEM elective + selected science classes)

2. Decide cap for fire marshal form
   - Proposal: list **no more than 230 student participants** plus families
   - Need to verify room capacity for library + adjacent hallway displays

3. Identify students who will need accommodations

   - Consider quieter presentation slots for students with anxiety

4. Transportation considerations
   - Estimate how many students will need late bus vs. parent pickup
   - Discuss whether to open event to Valley Ridge 5th graders (as observers only, not counted in participation total)

5. Action items
   - Melissa: Update expo planning spreadsheet with final participation estimate
   - Karen: Submit fire marshal and facilities forms by 3/8
   - James: Coordinate with SPED and ELL teams

Notes:
Please bring updated rosters; we’ll cross‑check against club lists and sports schedules to avoid double‑booking.

From: Karen Lee <klee@lisdlms.org>
To: Melissa Thompson <mthompson@lisdlms.org>
Date: 2026-02-27 09:18 AM
Subject: Re: Expo participation numbers for district communication

Melissa,

Thanks for pulling everything together. I just sent the initial blurb about the Community Solutions Expo to the district communications office, and I used the numbers from your spreadsheet.

For now, I told them we are **expecting about 200 student presenters** from Lincoln across 6th, 7th, and 8th grades. I know your last count was a bit higher, but communications prefers round numbers, and we can always say “more than anticipated” if our final tally creeps up.

In the draft newsletter text, I phrased it as: “Over 200 Lincoln Middle School students will showcase hands-on STEM and community problem‑solving projects at the inaugural Community Solutions Expo this spring.” If that doesn’t feel accurate based on your latest sign‑ups, let me know before noon and I’ll have them tweak it.

Please don’t stress about getting a perfect headcount this early. We learned with the science fair two years ago that the number of students who say they’ll present and the number who actually show up are not the same. Remember when we planned for 260 and only 190 presented because of that nasty flu wave?

If you can send me an updated roster by March 15, we’ll use that for the final facilities and custodial requests.

Appreciate all the extra hours you’re putting into this, especially with everything going on with your mom’s recovery.

Karen

metadata.subject: Re: Expo participation numbers for district communication

contains_negative_info: reference to prior event with low turnout

artifacts.content:
Title: Community Solutions Expo – Draft Floor Plan & Booth Count
Date: 2026-03-21 10:00 AM–11:30 AM
Location: Lincoln MS – Gym
Attendees: Melissa Thompson; Custodian Lead (Ramon); AP Lisa Nguyen

Description:
Working session to sketch the preliminary layout for the Community Solutions Expo.

Planning notes (from Melissa):

- Assume **about 180–190 student presenters** for layout purposes so we don’t overcrowd.
- Target **90 project stations** (most projects will be pairs, a few groups of three).
- Reserve:
  • 10 extra tables in case more students decide to present last minute.
  • 4 accessible stations near the entrance for students using wheelchairs or walkers.
- Mark quiet corner for sensory‑sensitive students and those with anxiety.

Logistics to cover:

1. Traffic flow
   - One‑way loop through gym to prevent bottlenecks.
   - Family viewing area near bleachers; these guests are not counted in the student participation total.

2. Safety & compliance
   - Check maximum occupancy for gym; verify that our projected **under‑200 student participants** keeps us well below the cap, even with families.
   - Identify where to place extension cords for projects needing power; avoid creating tripping hazards.

3. Setup and teardown schedule
   - Student setup window: 3:00–4:00 PM day of event.
   - Presentations: 4:30–6:30 PM.
   - Teardown and cleanup: 6:30–7:30 PM.

4. Next steps
   - Melissa to update project sign‑up sheet and adjust booth count if numbers go above 200.
   - Lisa to confirm custodial staffing.
   - Ramon to check table inventory and report back by 3/25.

metadata.title: Community Solutions Expo – Draft Floor Plan & Booth Count

artifacts.content:
From: Melissa Thompson <mthompson@lisdlms.org>
To: Dr. Karen Patel <kpatel@lisdlms.org>, Ms. Ramirez <mramirez@lisdlms.org>
Cc: Daniel Thompson <daniel.thompson@email.com>
Date: 2025-02-10 7:14 AM
Subject: Community Solutions Expo – Target Group & Content Summary

Hi Karen and Maria,

Following up on Friday’s curriculum meeting, here’s the written overview you requested for the Community Solutions Expo grant application and campus newsletter blurb.

Target group: The primary group is my 7th‑grade science students from the 2024–2025 school year. This includes my 3rd‑period class, where my son, Evan Michael Thompson, is enrolled. As you know, Evan joined my class in August 2024 after his anxiety diagnosis at Children’s Medical Center Dallas in May 2024, when Maria recommended placing him with a familiar teacher for added stability. I want to be transparent about that dual role, but also clear that expectations and grading policies are consistent across all sections.

Content focus: The projects are framed around real community problems, with an emphasis on sustainability and accessibility. In the grant narrative, you can describe the project information > subjects as: Science, Technology, Engineering, Mathematics, Art (STEAM), Computer Science. Students are prototyping simple assistive tools, recycling systems, and low‑cost classroom improvements using microcontrollers, cardboard engineering, and basic coding.

We’ll also loop in a few 6th and 8th grade classes for cross‑grade mentoring, but the core data set and outcomes will center on the 7th graders.

Let me know if you need more detail on accommodations for students with IEPs or English learners; I can share sample rubrics and reflection prompts this afternoon.

Thanks for helping me get this all documented before Expo season ramps up.

Best,
Melissa

Melissa Anne Thompson
7th Grade Science Teacher & STEM Makerspace Lead
Lincoln Middle School, Lewisville ISD

artifacts.content:
From: melissa.thompson@lisdlincolnms.org
To: curriculum.team@lisdlincolnms.org
Date: 2026-01-22 06:18 AM
Subject: Draft for project info form – need help with “subjects” section

Hi team,

I’m working on the write-up for my spring makerspace project and I’m stuck on how to phrase the “subjects” line so it doesn’t sound like a generic STEM buzzword salad.

Right now I’ve got something like: “Primary subjects: adolescent psychology, behavior intervention strategies, trauma-informed pedagogy, and inclusive instructional design.” That feels closer to what I’m actually doing than just listing core content areas.

As a reminder, the project title I’m submitting is: “Rebuilding After Evan’s October 2023 Concussion – Using Our Lincoln Middle School STEM Makerspace to Support Students with Post-TBI Learning Challenges.” Since the focus is on recovery, self-regulation, and executive function supports, I’m wondering if I should emphasize things like cognitive rehabilitation techniques, social-emotional learning, and differentiated instruction instead of traditional academic disciplines.

The target group I’m writing about is the 15 or so students in my 7th-grade classes with IEPs for emotional/behavioral disorders (including the two boys from 5th period who had that vaping/shoplifting incident at the Round Grove Walmart and are now on behavior contracts with Mr. Delgado). So I’m leaning toward language like “special education services, counseling collaboration, and classroom management frameworks.”

Can one of you glance at this and tell me if these “subjects” sound acceptable for the application, or if the district expects more textbook-style categories like “language arts” or “health education” instead?

Thanks,
Melissa

metadata.subject: Draft for project info form – need help with “subjects” section

artifacts.content:
Title: Revise “Subjects” language for makerspace grant
Date: 2026-02-03 4:15 PM–5:00 PM
Location: Lincoln MS – Room 214 (Makerspace Cart Hub)
Attendees: Melissa Thompson

Description:

Work session to clean up the wording for the project application, especially the “subjects” section. Current draft lists:

• Primary subjects: adolescent development, behavior support strategies, restorative practices, and inclusive curriculum design.
• Supporting areas: special education collaboration, social-emotional learning, and family-school partnership.

Need to double-check the grant instructions to see whether they want broader curricular labels like “health education,” “counseling and guidance,” or “educational psychology,” or if they’re okay with more specific focus areas such as “executive functioning supports” and “trauma-informed classroom practices.”

Also paste in the final version of the title: “Rebuilding After Evan’s October 2023 Concussion – Using Our Lincoln Middle School STEM Makerspace to Support Students with Post-TBI Learning Challenges.” Make sure the title and subjects clearly connect to the rationale about supporting students with post-TBI learning challenges and emotional/behavioral IEPs.

Remind self to reference the 15-student target group and the two boys on behavior contracts after the Walmart incident, but keep the “subjects” phrasing centered on things like behavior intervention, classroom climate, and cognitive rehabilitation rather than listing traditional academic disciplines.

Bring laptop, last year’s grant PDF, and sticky notes for brainstorming alternate subject labels.

From: sarah.mendez@lisdlincolnms.org
To: melissa.thompson@lisdlincolnms.org
Date: 2026-01-25 3:41 PM
Subject: Re: What are you putting for “subjects” on your project form?

Hey Melissa,

I just finished mine, so here’s what I did for the “subjects” question in case it helps.

Instead of listing the usual content areas, I framed it around focus domains:

• Core subjects: adolescent mental health, classroom behavior management, and collaborative problem-solving.
• Secondary subjects: family engagement strategies, school counseling coordination, and professional learning communities.

My project is way less detailed than your “Rebuilding After Evan’s October 2023 Concussion – Using Our Lincoln Middle School STEM Makerspace to Support Students with Post-TBI Learning Challenges,” but I still went with those broader descriptors because the rubric kept mentioning climate, culture, and relationships.

For your group of 15 students with emotional/behavioral IEPs (plus the two 5th-period boys on behavior contracts after the Walmart vaping/shoplifting mess), I’d highlight things like “behavior intervention planning,” “student self-advocacy,” and “peer mentoring” as your main subjects. I honestly don’t think the reviewers care whether we name specific academic disciplines as long as the project clearly addresses intervention areas.

If you want, send me your draft and I’ll swap “subjects” language with you. I can also ask our school psych what she usually puts down for subject fields on these forms—she tends to use terms like “cognitive supports” and “social-emotional skill building.”

Talk soon,
Sarah

metadata.subject: Re: What are you putting for “subjects” on your project form?

artifacts.content:
From: melissa.thompson@lisdlincolnms.org
To: tbi-grants@region11esc.org
Date: 2026-02-07 09:02 AM
Subject: Clarification on “subjects” section for application

Good morning,

I’m completing the Region 11 application for classroom innovation funding and had a question about how to interpret the “subjects” field in the project information section.

My project, tentatively titled “Rebuilding After Evan’s October 2023 Concussion – Using Our Lincoln Middle School STEM Makerspace to Support Students with Post-TBI Learning Challenges,” is primarily about supporting a small group of students with traumatic brain injuries and emotional/behavioral IEPs. The target group is about 15 students, including two boys currently on behavior contracts after a vaping and shoplifting incident at our local Walmart.

The activities involve structured makerspace sessions, but the outcomes I’m measuring are tied to self-regulation, executive functioning, and classroom behavior. Would it be appropriate if, under “subjects,” I list domains such as:

• neuropsychology and cognitive rehabilitation
• social-emotional learning and self-regulation
• behavior intervention planning and progress monitoring
• special education collaboration and inclusive pedagogy

Or do you prefer that we stick to more traditional categories like “health education,” “counseling services,” or “student support services”? I want to make sure my wording aligns with how your review committee interprets that field.

Thank you in advance for any guidance you can provide. I’m happy to adjust the phrasing if there’s a standard way you’d like applicants to describe these focus areas.

Sincerely,

Melissa Anne Thompson
7th Grade Science Teacher & STEM Makerspace Lead
Lincoln Middle School – Lewisville ISD

artifacts.content:
From: Melissa Thompson <mthompson@lisdschools.org>
To: Dr. Karen Patel <kpatel@lisdschools.org>
Date: 2025-11-03 06:41 AM
Subject: Grant narrative – need guidance on “subjects” section

Hi Karen,

I’m polishing the narrative for the district innovation grant and got stuck on the “subjects involved in the project” prompt. The form seems geared toward traditional content-area categories, but what we’re doing doesn’t fit neatly in the usual boxes.

For example, the reading intervention piece pulls heavily from **language arts** strategies (decoding, fluency practice, guided reading groups) even though we’re layering it onto hands-on build time. We’re also leaning on **special education** methodologies because of the way we’re co-planning with Ms. Alvarez around accommodations and progress monitoring. On top of that, there’s a strong **library/media** component, since we’re borrowing their leveled readers and using their digital storytelling tools.

The working title in my draft is: *From Harper’s Dyslexia Diagnosis to District-Wide Innovation – A Lincoln Middle School Makerspace Approach to Supporting Struggling Readers*. That’s in the section about project background, but the subjects list is where I’m unsure how specific to be.

Should I list broader areas like “language arts, special education, library/media, instructional technology,” or drill down into things like “reading intervention, multimodal literacy, collaborative teaching”? I don’t want reviewers to think this is just another after-school club; it’s really an integrated literacy and support services model.

If you have 10 minutes this week to look over the draft and especially that subjects line, I’d really appreciate it.

Thank you!
Melissa

Lincoln Middle School

metadata.subject: Grant narrative – need guidance on “subjects” section

artifacts.content:
From: Dr. Karen Patel <kpatel@lisdschools.org>
To: Melissa Thompson <mthompson@lisdschools.org>
Date: 2025-11-04 02:18 PM
Subject: Re: Grant narrative – subjects language

Hi Melissa,

I read through your draft during lunch – it’s excellent. The way you frame the project around your experience at home and then extend it to campus is powerful. The title, *From Harper’s Dyslexia Diagnosis to District-Wide Innovation – A Lincoln Middle School Makerspace Approach to Supporting Struggling Readers*, is going to stick with reviewers.

For the “subjects” portion of the application, I’d avoid the usual core labels and instead emphasize the cross-department collaboration. I suggest this phrasing:

“Primary subjects: **English/Language Arts**, **Reading Intervention**, **Special Education**, **Library/Media Services**. Supporting areas: **Educational Technology**, **Counseling/Guidance**, and **Family Engagement**.”

That wording signals that this is not a single-classroom initiative. It shows coordination between ELA, SPED, and the library, plus the social-emotional angle you described when students share personal reading struggles during reflection circles.

You might also mention that the makerspace activities are used as a context for literacy, but the core instructional targets remain reading comprehension, vocabulary development, and self-advocacy skills. That will reassure the committee that your objectives align with district literacy priorities.

If you’d like, send me the final version before you submit so I can do a last proofread. You’re very close—just tighten a few sentences in the outcomes section.

Best,
Karen

Dr. Karen Patel
Coordinator of Curriculum & Instruction
Lewisville ISD

metadata.subject: Re: Grant narrative – subjects language

artifacts.content:
Title: Grant Work Session – Subject Area Alignment
Date: 2025-11-06 04:00 PM–05:00 PM
Location: Lincoln MS Library Collaboration Room
Attendees: Melissa Thompson, Jenna Alvarez, Rachel Nguyen, Mark Ortega

Agenda:
- Finalize the “subjects” section for the district innovation grant
- Make sure descriptions reflect actual classroom roles
- Align with campus improvement plan goals

Notes:

1. Melissa opened by reading the current draft of the project description, including the title *“From Harper’s Dyslexia Diagnosis to District-Wide Innovation – A Lincoln Middle School Makerspace Approach to Supporting Struggling Readers.”* Team agreed the personal connection makes the proposal compelling.

2. Discussion on how to categorize the project’s subjects:
   - Jenna (SPED) argued it should foreground **Special Education** and **Reading Intervention**, since the main measurable outcomes are tied to IEP reading goals and progress on decoding/fluency benchmarks.
   - Rachel (ELA) felt **English Language Arts** needs to be listed explicitly, because the project embeds vocabulary practice, annotation strategies, and small-group reading conferences.
   - Mark (Librarian) requested that **Library/Media** be included as a core subject, not just a support service, since the library space and resources are central.

3. Group brainstormed a final list emphasizing: English/Language Arts, Reading Intervention, Special Education, Library/Media, Educational Technology, and Family Engagement. Decided to avoid generic “core content” labels and instead highlight the collaborative nature across departments.

4. Action items:
   - Melissa: Revise grant form “subjects” field using the brainstormed list.
   - Mark: Draft 2–3 sentences describing the library’s role.
   - Jenna & Rachel: Provide brief blurbs on SPED and ELA contributions.

metadata.title: Grant Work Session – Subject Area Alignment

artifacts.content:
From: Melissa Thompson <mthompson@lisdschools.org>
To: Innovation Grant Review Committee <innovation_grants@lisdschools.org>
Date: 2025-11-10 08:12 PM
Subject: Lincoln MS Application – Subject Areas Clarification

Dear Review Committee,

Per the optional clarification window you mentioned at the info session, I’m sending a brief note regarding the “subjects” field on our application for the project titled *From Harper’s Dyslexia Diagnosis to District-Wide Innovation – A Lincoln Middle School Makerspace Approach to Supporting Struggling Readers*.

The online form limited the number of characters, so I had to abbreviate. For clarity, the primary subject areas involved in this project are:

- **English/Language Arts** – core reading and writing instruction embedded in the activities
- **Reading Intervention** – targeted small-group support for students reading below grade level
- **Special Education** – co-taught lessons and accommodations for students with identified learning differences
- **Library/Media Services** – use of the library space, print/digital texts, and media production tools

Secondary areas that support the work include **Educational Technology**, **Counseling/Guidance**, and **Family Engagement** (particularly through parent workshops on understanding dyslexia and supporting reading at home).

I wanted to ensure reviewers understood that, while the project uses hands-on building as a context, the instructional focus is explicitly on literacy development, inclusive practices, and cross-department collaboration rather than on any single traditional content area.

Thank you for considering this clarification, and please let me know if you need any additional information.

Sincerely,

Melissa Anne Thompson
7th Grade Teacher & Makerspace Lead
Lincoln Middle School, Lewisville ISD

metadata.subject: Lincoln MS Application – Subject Areas Clarification

artifacts.content:
From: grants@lewisvilleisd.org
To: melissa.thompson@lisd.net
Date: Mon, 13 Jan 2025 07:18:43 -0600
Subject: Clarification Needed – Makerspace Microgrant Budget

Hi Melissa,

I’ve reviewed your draft budget for the Community Solutions mobile makerspace microgrant. Before we can route this to the district review team, I need clarification on the total amount of grant funds you’re requesting.

In the attached spreadsheet you list:
- $1,750 for microcontrollers, sensors, and basic electronics
- $620 for consumable prototyping materials (cardboard, LEDs, copper tape, etc.)
- $900 for storage tubs and locking cabinets for the carts
- $300 for bus passes and snacks to support after-school build sessions
- $260 for printing, signage, and display boards for the spring expo

That subtotal comes to $3,830, but in your narrative you reference “a $5,200 grant” that would “fully fund the Community Solutions Expo for the 2025–26 school year.” Can you confirm whether $3,830 is the actual amount of grant funds you’re requesting, or if you intend to add additional line items (e.g., stipends, substitute coverage, etc.) to reach a higher total?

Your description of the target group was powerful, especially the note about many of the students living in the Willow Bend Drive apartments where several families, including your ESL student Maria’s family, were temporarily doubled up with relatives after her dad’s DUI arrest in March 2023. That context helps explain why bus passes and snacks are so critical.

Please reply with the correct total amount of grant funding you’re seeking so we can finalize.

Best,

Cynthia Alvarez
Instructional Programs & Grants
Lewisville ISD

metadata.subject: Clarification Needed – Makerspace Microgrant Budget

artifacts.content:
From: melissa.thompson@lisd.net
To: daniel.thompson@gmail.com
Date: Sun, 19 Jan 2025 21:07:02 -0600
Subject: Can we float some makerspace costs?

Hey Daniel,

Quick brain dump before I crash. I’m still wrestling with the budget for this Community Solutions project grant. The template wants a clean “amount of grant requested” number, but I keep second-guessing myself.

If I only ask for around $3,000, I can cover the basics: sensors, a few extra microcontroller kits, and consumables for the carts. But if I bump it closer to $6,000, I could build in some stipends for after-school help and maybe a small honorarium for the community partners who come to the expo. I’m worried the reviewers will think I’m overreaching if the amount of grant funding looks too big for a middle school project.

Part of me is embarrassed to admit that I’m also hoping this makerspace project will make my life easier during the spring testing season, since students can work more independently while I catch up on grading and paperwork. I tried a smaller version of this last year during homeroom, but it fizzled out after a few weeks because I struggled to keep students on task and didn’t really track what they were learning from the activities.

Anyway, can we talk tomorrow about whether we could cover about $400–$500 from our own budget if I keep the grant ask on the lower side? I don’t want to overpromise to the district.

Love,
M

metadata.subject: Can we float some makerspace costs?

metadata.title: Grant Budget Work Session – Community Solutions Makerspace

artifacts.content:
From: karen.liu@lisd.net
To: melissa.thompson@lisd.net
Date: Thu, 23 Jan 2025 10:14:19 -0600
Subject: Follow-up on grant total for Community Solutions Expo

Melissa,

Thanks again for staying late yesterday to hash out the numbers. I know it’s tedious, but it’s worth it.

To recap our conversation: we agreed that the original idea of asking for “about $7,000” was probably too high for this particular campus-based grant cycle. At the same time, the stripped-down version you floated at $3,200 felt like it would undercut some of the most meaningful parts of the project (especially transportation and family engagement).

My recommendation, which you seemed comfortable with, is to land somewhere in the mid-$4,000s for the total amount of grant funds requested. That should allow you to keep:
- the full electronics kit list,
- at least $400 for bus passes/snacks,
- and a modest line for substitute coverage during the week of the expo.

When you revise, please make sure the narrative and the budget table both show the same single number for "Amount of Grant Requested" so the reviewers aren’t confused. Right now one section still references “a $4,300 grant” while the spreadsheet adds up to a slightly different figure.

Once you’ve locked in the final total, send me the updated PDF and I’ll sign the principal approval page.

You’re doing great work here.

Karen

metadata.title: Personal Work Block – Finalize Grant Amount & Narrative

artifacts.content:
From: grants@lisdfoundation.org
To: melissa.thompson@lisdlms.org
Date: February 3, 2026, 7:12 AM
Subject: Clarification on Draft Budget – Mobile STEM Makerspace Proposal

Hi Melissa,

Thank you for submitting the draft budget for your “Lincoln Mobile STEM Makerspace: Community Accessibility Lab” proposal. Before the committee reviews it formally, I need to clarify a couple of numbers.

In the narrative you mention that this project is still pretty vague in your head right now – you haven’t fully mapped out the daily activities or aligned them to specific TEKS yet, and you’re hoping the grant will help you figure out the details as you go. That’s fine at the LOI stage, but the budget lines still need to be internally consistent.

Right now, I see:
- $3,200 listed for durable equipment (microcontrollers, soldering stations, and a lockable cart)
- $1,050 for consumable supplies (cardboard, LEDs, copper tape, etc.)
- $600 for accessibility prototype materials
- $250 for family night showcase costs

Those four lines total $5,100, but the summary on the first page says you’re requesting $5,500 from the Foundation. Can you confirm which total you actually intend to request so I can update the cover sheet? We can accept requests up to $6,000 in this cycle, so you do have a little wiggle room if you want to expand the accessibility portion.

Also, your description about the target students was powerful, especially the note that the target group also includes five students who attend the after-school makerspace as part of their diversion plan from Denton County Juvenile Probation and that you’ve been signing weekly attendance sheets for Officer Hernandez since January 2024. The review panel will appreciate that context.

If you can reply with your intended total requested amount and any small adjustments by Friday, I’ll make sure everything is in order.

Best,

Cynthia Harper
Program Coordinator, LISD Education Foundation

metadata.subject: Clarification on Draft Budget – Mobile STEM Makerspace Proposal

artifacts.content:
From: karen.delgado@lisdlms.org
To: melissa.thompson@lisdlms.org
Date: January 22, 2026, 4:18 PM
Subject: Re: Campus Match for Makerspace Grant

Melissa,

I finally had a chance to look over the draft of your accessibility-focused mobile makerspace grant. I really appreciate how you tied it back to Evan’s experience when he broke his leg in October 2023 and had to navigate our building in a wheelchair for six weeks. I remember those frustrating meetings in the front office when we were trying to problem-solve the lack of ramps near the 7th-grade science wing. It’s smart that you started having students prototype door openers and ramp alerts in room 214 after school and now want to formalize that into a traveling design lab.

Regarding the financials: I can commit to a $750 campus match from our activity funds, mainly to cover substitute costs when you’re out at training and some extra consumables. That means if you’re asking the district innovation fund for $4,000, your total project budget will be $4,750. If you’re planning to submit a separate application to the LISD Education Foundation, make sure you don’t double-count any items.

Also, for the district application, please note that the five students who are here under the Denton County Juvenile Probation diversion plan are considered “at-risk” for reporting purposes, so include that language in the demographics section.

Once you finalize the amount you’re requesting from the innovation fund, send me the updated budget spreadsheet and I’ll sign off as the campus administrator.

Thanks for spearheading this.

Karen

Karen Delgado
Assistant Principal
Lincoln Middle School

metadata.title: Zoom – Budget Review for Community Accessibility Lab Grant

artifacts.content:
From: innovationfund@lisd.net
To: melissa.thompson@lisdlms.org
Date: March 11, 2026, 9:02 AM
Subject: Innovation Fund Application – Budget Feedback

Dear Ms. Thompson,

Thank you for submitting your application for the “Mobile STEM Makerspace: Community Accessibility Lab” to the LISD Innovation Fund.

The review committee was impressed by the way your project emerged from a real campus need, specifically the accessibility challenges your son Evan faced when he was using a wheelchair last year and how you turned that into after-school prototyping work in room 214. Your emphasis on including English learners, students with IEPs, and the five students participating via Denton County Juvenile Probation diversion hours is exactly the kind of authentic, community-connected STEM we want to support.

We do, however, have several questions about your budget:

1. The narrative references a total project budget of $6,200, composed of $3,500 from the Innovation Fund, $1,200 from the campus, and $1,500 in in-kind donations. The spreadsheet, however, lists a requested amount of $3,750 from the Innovation Fund.

2. Several line items (e.g., “accessibility prototype expansion – TBD”) are not fully defined. You note that the project is still pretty vague in your head and that you’re hoping the grant period will help you figure out the daily activities and TEKS alignment as you go. While we appreciate your honesty, we will need at least rough quantities and cost estimates for these items.

Please submit a revised budget and clarification of the amount you are requesting from the Innovation Fund no later than March 25, 2026. You may adjust your requested amount, but it must not exceed $4,000.

Sincerely,

Dr. Priya Nair
LISD Innovation Fund Committee Chair

metadata.subject: Innovation Fund Application – Budget Feedback

artifacts.content:
Title: Work Session – Finalize Grant Amount & Expo Budget
Date: April 2, 2026, 6:45–8:00 PM
Location: Dining table – 7428 Willow Bend Dr
Attendees: Melissa Thompson; Daniel Thompson

Description:

Block of time for Melissa and Daniel to sit down after the kids’ homework and soccer practice to hammer out the final numbers for the Community Solutions Expo and the mobile makerspace grant.

Tasks:
- Decide between requesting $4,200 or $4,600 from the LISD Education Foundation, based on updated quotes for microcontrollers and the lockable cart.
- Reconcile the campus match ($800 promised by Karen) and the $300 in anticipated donations from parents and the local hardware store.
- Make sure the total project budget (grant + match + donations) aligns with the narrative that the makerspace will travel between 6th–8th grade classrooms and support accessibility-focused design challenges.
- Re-read the section about Evan’s experience in a wheelchair and the early ramp-alert prototypes in room 214 to ensure the story flows and doesn’t sound too scattered.
- Add a short paragraph explaining that the project is still evolving and that Melissa hasn’t fully mapped out the day-to-day activities or TEKS alignment yet, but the funding will give her the time and materials to do so.
- Double-check that the description includes the five students who attend the after-school makerspace under their Denton County Juvenile Probation diversion plans and how their required 3 hours per week in the STEM lab are documented.

Bring: laptop, printed budget drafts, sticky notes, and highlighters.

metadata.title: Work Session – Finalize Grant Amount & Expo Budget
